stories.title: A new study finds 76% of firms think they are ahead on AI, while 10% are actually getting a return
stories.summary: EXL's 2026 enterprise AI study found that 76 percent of companies believe they are beating competitors on AI, while only one in ten have embedded it into core work and seen real returns. The firms that pulled ahead did not buy better tools, they redesigned how the work is done. The useful question for a leader is not which model to add but which workflow you would rebuild if AI had been there from the start.

stories.title: Your AI vendor can change the model under you with no warning, and OpenAI just published the proof
stories.summary: OpenAI published a method that replays around 1.3 million real conversations through a new model before release, built specifically to catch the behaviour changes that ship silently with model updates. The lesson for any firm that has put an AI step into client work is that a process you tested and trusted can drift without notice. Keep a person checking the judgement-heavy output, and re-test on real tasks when something starts to feel different.

stories.title: Prompt injection is being called a permanent flaw in AI agents, not a bug that gets patched
stories.summary: As firms wire AI agents into email and documents, security researchers warn that the trick of hiding instructions in the content an agent reads may not be fully fixable. Treat an agent like a junior with no supervision: do not give it standing access to anything you would not let an unsupervised newcomer touch.

stories.title: Anthropic says Claude now writes more than 80% of its code, a useful prompt to discount vendor numbers
stories.summary: Anthropic reports that more than 80 percent of the code merged into its production codebase is now written by its own model, up from low single digits last year. The figure is a share of work done, not a productivity multiple, and the two are easy to confuse. Judge any AI tool by what it does for work like yours, not by a vendor's headline adoption statistic.

stories.title: A frontier model was switched off worldwide overnight, a reminder you borrow AI rather than own it
stories.summary: A US export-control order pulled Anthropic's two most capable models for every customer globally on a Friday evening, with no restoration date. The lesson for a leader is operational, not political: find where one outside model has quietly become load-bearing in client work, and keep a tested alternative so nothing important stops if it disappears.

stories.title: OpenAI's GPT-5.6 is close, billed as a meaningful step up, but not yet shipped
stories.summary: OpenAI's next flagship, GPT-5.6, is being described by its chief scientist as a meaningful leap and is expected within weeks. A new top model is worth noting, but there is no decision to make until it ships and you can test it on your own work. Watch for the release rather than acting on the billing.

stories.title: Google's Gemini 3.5 Pro is still pending, with a much larger context window on the way
stories.summary: Gemini 3.5 Pro, promised with a two-million-token context window and a deeper reasoning mode, has not shipped yet, with market odds pointing to late June. A bigger context window matters for long-document work such as diligence and contract review, so it is worth watching, but it is not a decision to make until it is actually available.
